coredata.dc:title: Effects of plastic-derived carbon dots on germination and growth of pea (Pisum sativum) via seed nano-priming
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Lili
Wong, Siew Cheong
Lisak, Grzegorz
coredata.dc:description: Seed nano-priming is a promising technology employed in the agronomic field to promote seed germination and plant growth. However, the effects of carbon dots (CDs) on plant development via seed nano-priming remain unclear. In the present study, CDs synthesized from non-biodegradable plastic wastes were adopted as a nano-priming agent for pea (Pisum sativum) seed treatment. The results demonstrated positive effects of seed priming at all CD concentrations (0.25–2 mg/mL), including accelerated seed germination rate, increased shoot and root elongation, biomass accumulation, and root moisture level compared to the control groups. Surface erosion of seed coat was observed after CD priming, which effectively promoted seed imbibition capability. CD penetration, internalization, and translocation were confirmed using transmission electron microscopy. Furthermore, the CD-plant interaction significantly enhanced seed antioxidant enzyme activity, as well as augmented root vigor, chlorophyll content, and carbohydrate content. These findings exhibit great potential of waste-derived CDs as nano-priming agents for seed germination and seedling development in a cost-effective and sustainable manner.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of CDs. (a) TEM figure of CDs. (b) Hydrodynamic diameter of CD solution (0.75 mg/mL). (c) Normalized FTIR spectra of char and CDs. (d) Zeta potential of CD solution (0.75 mg/mL). (e) XRD pattern of CDs. (f) Full-scan XPS spectrum and high-resolution (g) C1s and (h) O1s XPS spectra of CDs. Inset in (a) shows the size distribution of CDs.

Effects of CD priming on seed germination. (a) Photograph of pea seeds primed with CDs of 0.75 mg/mL for 9 h. (b) Figure of CD (0.75 mg/mL) treated pea seeds after incubation for 36 h. (c) Germination rate of pea seeds primed with CDs of different concentrations (0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, and 2 mg/mL). (d) Representative photograph of germinated pea seeds after priming with CD solution (0.75 mg/mL) and Milli-Q water.

Effects of CD priming on the surface wettability and water uptake capacity of pea seeds. (a) SEM figure of the surface of pea seeds after water priming for 9 h. (b) SEM figure of the surface of pea seeds after CD (0.75 mg/mL) priming for 9 h. (c) Contact angle of seed coat surface after priming. (d) Mass loss curves of dry seeds and seeds primed with water and CD solution (0.75 mg/mL) for 9 h. Insets in (a) and (b) display the water droplet deposited on the surface of pea seeds primed with water and CD solution, respectively. Inset in (d) shows the moisture level of pea seeds after priming treatment (dry seed as a reference). Error bars correspond to standard deviation. The symbols “**” (P < 0.01) and “****” (P < 0.0001) indicate significant differences from control.

Effects of CD priming on pea seedling growth. Shoot and root (a) length, (b) diameter, (c) fresh weight (FW), (d) dry weight (DW), (e) root moisture level, and (f) shoot moisture level of pea seedlings after seed priming with water and CD solution. (g) Photograph of pea seedling morphology after CD treatment at different concentrations (0–2 mg/mL) Error bars correspond to standard deviation. The symbols “*” (P < 0.05), “**” (P < 0.01), and “***” (P < 0.001) indicate significant differences from control, respectively.

TEM figures of seed coats (a, f), embryos (b, g), roots (c, h), shoots (d, i), and leaves (e, j) of pea primed with water and CD solution (0.75 mg/mL), respectively. Arrows indicate the location of CD accumulation.

Antioxidant enzyme activity of pea seed embryos under different treatments. (a) SOD, (b) POD, (c) APX, and (d) CAT activity of pea seeds after priming with water and CD solution for 9 h, respectively. Error bars correspond to standard deviation. The symbols “*” (P < 0.05), “**” (P < 0.01), and “***” (P < 0.001) indicate significant differences from control, respectively.

Effects of CD priming on the biochemical parameters of pea seedlings. (a) Root vigor, (b) carbohydrate content, and (c) chlorophyll content of pea seedlings after seed priming with water and CD solution. Error bars correspond to standard deviation. The symbols “*” (P < 0.05), and “***” (P < 0.001) indicate significant differences from control, respectively.

head.ce:title: Effects of plastic-derived carbon dots on germination and growth of pea (Pisum sativum) via seed nano-priming

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Seed nano-priming is a promising technology employed in the agronomic field to promote seed germination and plant growth. However, the effects of carbon dots (CDs) on plant development via seed nano-priming remain unclear. In the present study, CDs synthesized from non-biodegradable plastic wastes were adopted as a nano-priming agent for pea (Pisum sativum) seed treatment. The results demonstrated positive effects of seed priming at all CD concentrations (0.25–2 mg/mL), including accelerated seed germination rate, increased shoot and root elongation, biomass accumulation, and root moisture level compared to the control groups. Surface erosion of seed coat was observed after CD priming, which effectively promoted seed imbibition capability. CD penetration, internalization, and translocation were confirmed using transmission electron microscopy. Furthermore, the CD-plant interaction significantly enhanced seed antioxidant enzyme activity, as well as augmented root vigor, chlorophyll content, and carbohydrate content. These findings exhibit great potential of waste-derived CDs as nano-priming agents for seed germination and seedling development in a cost-effective and sustainable manner.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an indispensable component in human society, agriculture is still facing severe challenges in the 21st century, including global climate change, environmental contamination, and natural resource exhaustion (Usman et al., 2020). Moreover, the world population is projected to reach 9.7 billion by 2050 (Dubey et al., 2021), and the global food demand is expected to surge by 35%–56% from 2010 to 2050 (van Dijk et al., 2021). Therefore, innovative technologies must be integrated into traditional farming to meet the goal of sustainable agriculture where “zero hunger” is achieved (Usman et al., 2020). Nanotechnology is an emerging technology with wide-ranging applications in the fields of medicine, energy, environment, and agriculture (Kargozar and Mozafari, 2018). In December 2002, the United States Department of Agriculture announced the world's first draft of “roadmap” for employing nanotechnology in agricultural and food industries (Younis et al., 2021). Over the past few years, various nanomaterials (NMs) have been developed for agricultural practices, serving as fertilizers, pesticides, sensors, or growth regulators to ensure the quality and productivity of plants (Singh et al., 2019; Usman et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2021). Seed nano-priming is an advanced strategy derived from nanotechnology that can facilitate seed germination, enhance seedling development and possibly strengthen stress tolerance by utilizing NMs as priming agents prior to sowing (Nile et al., 2022). Considering that large amounts of NMs are required as growth medium or foliar spray solution when they are applied during the entire plant growth cycle (Xin et al., 2022), seed nano-priming may serve as an alternative approach for sustainable agronomic practices. Because nano-priming agents are simply applied during the pre-sowing stage accompanied with limited plant internalization when compared with long-term treatments of NMs (Wohlmuth et al., 2022). Nano-priming strategy could theoretically alleviate potential environmental exposure to NMs, especially human/animal exposure to NMs via plant ingestion (Kranjc and Drobne, 2019). Amongst diverse NMs, metal and metal oxide nanoparticles (NPs) are the most used NMs for seed nano-priming. For example, green synthesized gold NPs using galanga rhizome extracts were adopted as nano-priming agent to promote the germination percentage of aged maize seeds and also augmented seedling vigor index by three times than control (Mahakham et al., 2016). Besides, carbon-based NMs, including fullerenes (Shafiq et al., 2019), graphenes (López-Vargas et al., 2020), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) (González-García et al., 2022), and carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) (Baz et al., 2020), were successfully exploited in seed priming to enhance germination and growth-related traits of plants.

Carbon dots (CDs), likewise, is a new generation of CNPs with particle sizes of less than 10 nm. They also possess great potential in agriculture applications, owning to their attractive characteristics such as ultrasmall and uniform size, controllable photoluminescence, tunable functionalization, high hydrophilicity, facile cell permeation, and benign biocompatibility (Li et al., 2020b). Recently, a growing body of evidence has demonstrated the positive effects of CDs on plant growth, including mung beans (Li et al., 2016a; Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018a), coriander, garlic (Chakravarty et al., 2015), lettuce (Zheng et al., 2017), wheat (Tripathi and Sarkar, 2015), rice plants (Li et al., 2018, 2021), tomato (Kou et al., 2021), and Arabidopsis thaliana (Chen et al., 2020), by mixing CDs in the growth medium or foliar spray agent during the plant growth period. However, the use of CDs as a nano-priming agent is still scarce, and the investigation of CD-plant interaction mechanisms regarding different types of CDs and plant species is insufficient. CDs modified with putrescine were applied as a chemical priming agent for two-year-old grapevine cutting, exhibiting salt stress alleviation effect after irrigating the plant for 48 h (Gohari et al., 2021). Nevertheless, this study only demonstrated the effect of CDs on grapevine while the function of CDs as a seed nano-priming agent remains unexplored. Most recently, CDs derived from biomass and plastic wastes were synthesized and successfully promoted the germination index and root elongation of chickpeas, barley, mung beans, and wheat seeds (Chauhan et al., 2022). Although this study preliminarily showed the beneficial influence of CDs on seed germination via nano-priming, the underlying mechanism of CD priming effect is not fully investigated and how this treatment can promote further seedling development is yet to be confirmed. Moreover, despite the fact that CDs were intensively reported as biocompatible NMs with low toxicity, some studies have initially pointed out the potential risk of CDs (Yan et al., 2015; Yao et al., 2018). CDs synthesized using citric acid and ethylenediamine were used as carbon precursors and their toxicity upon different aquatic organisms was investigated. The growth of phytoplankton (Scenedesmus obliquus) was inhibited under CD treatment with a 96-h EC50 value of 74.8 mg/L (Yao et al., 2018). Hence, additional studies are required to evaluate the priming impact of CDs with various physicochemical properties towards different types of plant varieties.

In the present study, CDs were fabricated using waste polyethylene terephthalate (PET) bottles as the carbon source. Considering the long-lasting negative environmental impact imposed by non-biodegradable plastics, valorization of exhausted plastics into functional CDs offers a sustainable manner for waste volume reduction and environmental pollution alleviation (de Oliveira and da Silva Abreu, 2021). Similar with our previous study (Liang et al., 2021), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)-assisted hydrothermal method was carried out as a facile and eco-friendly approach for CD synthesis without hazardous chemical emissions during the reaction. Based on the beneficial effects of CDs on plant growth reported in other studies (Li et al., 2020b), it is expected that plastic-derived CDs may hold great potential as a seed nano-priming agent for building sustainable agriculture. Pea (Pisum sativum) is a nutrient-rich plant commonly found in people's diets worldwide. The fast-growing property and apparent phenotypic traits of pea seedlings enable research observation within a short growth period. Several studies have reported the effects of different NMs on the growth of pea plants (Chourasiya et al., 2021; Rahman et al., 2020). For example, nano-SiO2 and Gibberellic acid loaded nano-SiO2 showed positive effects on pea seed germination and seedling growth via nano-priming (Chourasiya et al., 2021). Conversely, platinum NPs stabilized with polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were reported to have inhibitory effects on both pea seed germination and pea plant growth by decreasing AMF and Rhizobial colonization (Rahman et al., 2020). To the best of the authors' knowledge, the effects of CD treatment on the growth of pea haven't been proposed yet. Therefore, pea was selected as a model plant in this study to observe the nano-priming effect of plastic-derived CDs on seed germination and seedling development. This study also revealed the distribution of CDs inside pea seeds and seedlings using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). To further unravel the positive influence of CD nano-priming, measurements of biochemical parameters including antioxidant enzyme activities, root vigor, chlorophyll content, and carbohydrate content were also carried out.

Green pea seeds (brand name: Malika Dry Green Peas) were produced in India and purchased from local manufacturer Dashmesh Singapore Pte Ltd in Singapore. All the chemicals were of analytical grade, used as received without further purification, and purchased from commercial suppliers (Sigma-Aldrich, Merck, and Electron Microscopy Sciences).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were obtained by H2O2-assisted hydrothermal method using waste PET bottles as a carbon precursor. In a typical procedure, 5 g of waste PET bottles were manually cut into small pieces and placed inside a horizontal tube furnace. Pyrolytic char was obtained after thermal treatment at 400 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 10 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. The obtained char was further ground to fine powder via ball milling at 300 rpm for 2 h. Then, 50 mg of char was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) containing 30 mL 2% H2O2 solution and reacted at 110 °C for 15 h. The produced CDs were centrifuged, filtered, and dialyzed for purification. After freeze-drying, around 5 mg CD powder was finally obtained and stored for further use. The synthesized CDs were characterized using TEM (JEOL JEM-1400 Plus), Zetasizer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Shimadzu IRPrestige-21) equipped with attenuated total reflectance sampling accessories, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Supra), and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance).

Green pea seeds were selected with nearly the same size and plumpness and then sterilized by soaking them in 5% NaClO solution for 10 min. Subsequently, the seeds were washed with Milli-Q water three times to remove the chemical residue and then immersed in Milli-Q water or CD solutions with different concentrations (0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 2 mg/mL) in 50 mL beakers. Each beaker contained 30 seeds with a proportion of seed weight to priming solution volume as 1:4 g/mL, and each group was repeated three times. The beakers were placed on a mechanical shaker (150 rpm) under dark condition at room temperature to ensure the seeds were in full contact with priming solution. After 9 h priming, the seeds were gently rinsed with water, loosely wrapped in a paper towel moisturized with 10 mL Milli-Q water inside a sterile Petri dish (90 mm × 15 mm), and kept in a dark environment at 25 °C for 36 h. A seed is considered germinated when the emerged radical reaches 2 mm (ISTA, 2022). The germination rate was recorded every 6 h until 36 h when all the seeds were germinated. The surface morphology of pea seeds after priming was observed with field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7200F). To determine seed wettability, the contact angle of seed coat surface was measured using a goniometer (OCA 15EC) after the primed seeds were dried in an oven at 70 °C for 72 h. The moisture content of primed seeds and dry seeds was determined through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) by heating from 30 °C to 250 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min and maintaining at 250 °C for 2 h under N2 atmosphere (Khodakovskaya et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2018).

For post-germination growth, the seeds were then transferred to a 1 mL pipette tip box filled with Milli-Q water, where each seed was placed above the hole and the root was vertically immersed in water. The boxes were exposed to 12h light (mixed red and blue LED light, 3000 lux)/12 h dark at 20–22 °C under 79–85% humidity, and pea seedlings were harvested after 10 d. Germination rate, main root and shoot length and diameter, fresh weight and dry weight of seedlings were measured and recorded.

To observe the internalization and translocation of CDs inside pea seeds and seedlings, ultrathin sections (70–100 nm) of plant tissue were obtained using ultramicrotome (Leica EM UC7) and observed with TEM (JEOL JEM-1400 Plus) at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV.
The measurement of root dehydrogenase can be used to determine root vigor through 2, 3, 5-Triphenyltetrazolium chloride colorimetric method (Wang et al., 2018).

Carbohydrates can be oxidized by sulfuric acid and transform into furfural or its derivatives, which can react with anthrone to form blue-green substance that shows maximum absorbance at 620 nm. In this study, leaves from pea seedlings were collected for carbohydrate content measurement according to the previously reported methodology (Wang et al., 2018).

After growing for 10 d, the chlorophyll content of fully expanded uppermost leaves from pea sprouts was determined by Chlorophyll Content Meter CCM-300 (Opti-Sciences). Five different points were measured from each side of the leave midrib, and the experiment was repeated three times to ensure sufficient accuracy.

Pea seeds (0.5 g) primed with H2O and CDs were separately homogenized using a pre-cooled mortar and pestle in 5 mL phosphate buffer (50 mM, pH 7.8) containing 0.1 mM EDTA, 1% (w/v) PVP and 0.5% (v/v) Triton X-100. After filtration and centrifugation at 12000g for 20 min at 4 °C, the supernatant was taken and stored on ice for antioxidant enzyme assay. Superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity was measured based on 50% inhibition of nitroblue tetrazolium reduction according to Cavalcanti et al. (2004). Catalase (CAT) activity was determined by recording the reduction of absorbance at 240 nm due to H2O2 degradation in the presence of CAT (Aebi, 1984). Ascorbate peroxidase (APX) activity was assayed by measuring the decreased absorbance of ascorbate at 290 nm (Nakano and Asada, 1981). Peroxidase (POD) activity was assessed through guaiacol oxidation method by measuring the increased absorbance at 470 nm due to the formation of tetraguaiacol (Li et al., 1998).

All the measurements were taken in triplicate, and the results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Tukey test (P < 0.05) was performed for statistical analysis of experimental data.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, CDs derived from PET waste possess a nearly circular shape and a uniform size of 2.5 ± 0.5 nm within the range of 1.3–4.0 nm (based on measurements of 261 particles). Besides, the dynamic light scattering measurement of CDs displayed in Fig. 1b demonstrates an average hydrodynamic size of 2.7 nm (PdI = 0.158), which is aligned with the TEM result and confirms the homogeneity of fabricated CDs. To reveal the surface chemistry of CDs, FTIR spectroscopy was adopted in the study. As shown in Fig. 1c, both char and CDs share similar absorption peaks. Specifically, the typical broad absorption band locates at 3395 cm−1 stands for O–H stretching vibrations (Mewada et al., 2014). The strong peaks at 1710 cm−1 and 1230 cm−1 are allocated to CO and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively (Jayaweera et al., 2019). The peak at 1580 cm−1 corresponds to CC stretching vibration and the peak at 1425 cm−1 is assigned to C–H bending vibration (Ţucureanu et al., 2016). The above results indicate the presence of oxygen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups on the surface of char and CDs. The functional groups from char surface are formed primarily due to the thermal decomposition of the long polymer chain of PET during pyrolysis process. Compared with the normalized FTIR spectrum of char, CD spectrum exhibits increased intensity of peaks involving oxygen-containing groups (O–H, CO, and C–O bonds), indicating higher oxidation degree of CD surface attributed to H2O2-assited hydrothermal reaction (Zhang et al., 2015b). Similar changes of FTIR spectra were also reported in other studies to demonstrate the introduction of functional groups after oxidation treatment (Li et al., 2020a; Pradhan and Sandle, 1999). Besides, the zeta potential of CDs in Fig. 1d showed a negative surface charge (−36.20 mV), which is resulted from the deprotonation of hydrophilic functional groups on the CD surface. CD solution with such a zeta potential is considered to possess high dispersion stability in aqueous systems (Clogston and Patri, 2011). The XRD pattern (Fig. 1e) of CDs shows two diffraction peaks located at around 14.9° and 23.3°. According to Bragg's Law, the interlayer spacing of the broad peak at 23.3° was calculated to be 0.38 nm, which is higher than that of graphitic carbon (d 002 = 0.35 nm) (JCPDS file No. 75–1621). The small peak at 14.9° is shifted from the peak corresponding to (002) plane in graphitic carbon, resulting from the introduction of oxygen-containing groups and water molecules to the carbon layers (Siburian et al., 2018). On the whole, the broad peak shape and peak location from XRD pattern demonstrate the amorphous nature of CDs and the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups. XPS is another technique to characterize the surface chemical composition of CDs. Fig. 1f presents peaks of C1s (283 eV) and O1s (530 eV), occupying 69.4% and 30.6% atomic percentage, respectively, after peak area integration. The C/O atomic ratio of both CDs and char were also calculated and displayed in Table S1 as 2.3 and 7.3, representing higher surface oxidation degree of CDs, which is also consistent with the CHNS elemental analysis result (Table S2). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) was deconvoluted into three peaks of C–C at 284.5 eV, C–O at 286.2 eV, and CO at 288.6 eV, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) was fitted into two peaks of CO and C–O at 531.5 eV and 532.8 eV, respectively. The above results further revealed the presence of oxygen-containing groups, complementing the FTIR results. According to the above findings, the possible mechanism of CDs synthesis via H2O2-assisted hydrothermal reaction is proposed as follows. Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) dissociated from H2O2 is one of the most efficient oxidizing species, which could break down the C–C and CC bonds of char. The resulting periphery carbons were subsequently oxidized into oxygen-containing groups (Hu et al., 2016). Attributed to high-temperature and high-pressure hydrothermal process as well as the presence of powerful •OH, char experienced dehydration, fragmentation, oxidation, and carbonization reactions, leading to the formation of CDs in this study.

To evaluate whether CD priming can promote seed germination and seedling development, green pea seeds were selected as the model plant. Pea seeds were primed in CD solutions of different concentrations (0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 2 mg/mL) and Milli-Q water (control group), respectively. It was found that all the groups of pea seeds germinated successfully within 2 d, thus observations were carried out each 6 h to compare the seed germination rate until 36 h after sowing in Petri dishes. Fig. 2 a and b are representative figures of CD primed pea seeds before and after germination in the Petri dishes. As shown in Fig. 2c, the water primed seeds held the lowest germination rate while CD solutions of all concentrations, especially the group of 0.75 mg/mL, were more efficient in promoting seed germination. After sowing for only 6 h, half of the pea seeds primed with 0.75 mg/mL CD solution germinated with distinct radicle protrusion, while the control group only showed relatively low germination rate at 14%. Besides, the pea seeds treated with CD solutions of 0.25, 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/mL exhibited similar germination rates during the observation period, but were obviously higher than that of the control group within the first 18 h after sowing. Moreover, a significant difference regards initial root length could be virtually observed between the germinated seeds treated with CDs (0.75 mg/mL) and pure water (Fig. 2d). After 36 h of germination, the radical of pea seeds from control group appeared visibly shorter and finer when compared with the ones under CD treatment. Overall, these results proved that CDs are capable of achieving rapid pea seed germination through seed nano-priming, consequently laying the foundation for seedling development.

To investigate the factors that potentially affect seed germination after priming, firstly, seed coat surface morphology and wettability as well as seed water uptake capability were assessed. SEM figures of seed coat were used to verify the effect of CD treatment (0.75 mg/mL) on the surface morphology. It was found that pea seeds treated with water still possessed intact surface structure (Fig. 3 a) while those treated with CDs underwent dramatic changes in the surface topography (Fig. 3b). Compared with the barely undamaged seed coat from the control group, the CD primed seeds turned out to have an eroded surface with distinct wrinkles, holes, and cracks, indicating partial destruction of the hydrophobic surface waxy layer (Zhou et al., 2016). The water contact angle was then recorded to reflect the surface wettability of the seed coat (Fig. 3a and b inset and Fig. 3c). In general, surfaces with a contact angle larger than 90° are considered hydrophobic while those with a contact angle less than 90° are described as hydrophilic. In this study, CD primed seeds possess a notably smaller contact angle (86°) than that of the control group (108°). The results indicated that water priming maintained the hydrophobic nature of the surface waxy layer, while CD priming contributed to a significant increase in surface wettability due to the modification of surface chemistry and morphological change. Besides, the moisture level of pea seeds after 9 h priming was determined via TGA technique, and dry pea seeds without any treatment were also measured as a reference. As shown in Fig. 3d, the mass loss curve of CD primed seeds was lower than that of H2O primed seeds. According to the drop of mass loss curve, the moisture level of dry pea seeds, seeds primed with H2O and CDs were calculated as 41.4%, 61.9%, and 65.6%, respectively (Fig. 3d inset). There is a significant increase (P < 0.01) in the moisture content of pea seeds primed with CD solution when compared with the control, demonstrating the favorable effect of CDs on enhancing the water uptake capability of pea seeds. Hence, it is believed that CD priming can cause partial damage to the waxy layer of seed testa, contributing to the enhancement of surface wettability, which is partially responsible for the elevated seed water uptake capability. Previous studies have demonstrated that the increased water uptake of seeds primed with nanomaterials, e.g. multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Joshi et al., 2018), is responsible for faster seed germination and higher seedling vigor. Comparably, it is expected that the improved seed imbibition observed in the current study would facilitate the seed germination process and promote seedling development.

To further understand the changes that occurred during the priming process, the pH value, electrical conductivity, and element composition of priming solution were measured accordingly. As shown in Fig. S1a, pH values of water and CD solution before and after priming treatment were recorded. Before priming, the pH values of CD solutions slightly dropped from 3.2 to 2.5 when the concentrations increased from 0.25 mg/mL to 2 mg/mL, which is apparently lower than that of Milli-Q water at 6.0. The acidic environment created by CDs was most likely attributed to the presence of abundant acidic functional groups, such as carboxyl groups, on the surface of CDs (Wang et al., 2019), consequently leading to the destruction of seed coat surface structure. After priming, the pH value of water decreased marginally while that of CD solution had a noticeable increase, which may be due to the presence of seed leachate and its’ interaction with CD surface functional groups. Besides, elemental analysis of pea seeds and priming solution was performed, and the major element components were displayed in Tables S3–4. It was found that all the cations measured in the priming solution showed an ascending trend with the increase of CD concentrations, and potassium ions (K+) were found in the seed leachate at the highest content. Thus, the change of K+ concentration in different priming solutions was selected as a representative for the remaining cations in Fig. S1b. Theoretically, under such circumstances, the electrical conductivity (EC) of priming solution should face a gradual increase as well. Conversely, there is a reduction of EC with the increase of CD concentrations, suggesting some of the leached cations may be bound during their interaction with CD surface. For example, ferric ions (Fe3+) (Dutta Chowdhury and Doong, 2016), K+ (Zhang et al., 2015a), and copper ions (Cu2+) (Gedda et al., 2016) may be involved as they are frequently reported to exhibit high affinity with CDs through complexation with the active sites from CD surface, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. In addition, Wang et al. reported that with the increase of CD concentration, in other words with increased surface sites in the system, the formation of deprotonated CD-Fe3+ complex is also growing (Wang et al., 2019). This finding agrees well with the pH and EC change occurred in this study. Nevertheless, further investigation is yet required to disclose the inner relationship between CD surface and seed leachate through accurate quantification of active surface sites from CDs and their interaction with specific ions or other moieties leached from seeds.

It has been reported that some CDs showed inhibition effects on algae growth and the decrease in the pH value of culture medium might be one of the reasons that explained the underlying toxicity mechanism (Yan et al., 2015; Yao et al., 2018). In the current study, although pea seeds were washed after priming to remove additional CDs on the surface, inevitable release of CDs into the environment may occur during seed germination and plant growth. Considering the acidic environment created by CDs during seed priming that partially caused seed coat erosion, it is also worth concerning the potential environmental risk posed by CDs. Additional research is needed to identify the toxic impact of plastic-derived CDs on low-trophic level organisms such as microorganisms and phytoplankton, which play essential roles in the soil and aquatic ecosystem and build the basis of the entire food chain.

It is expected that the increase of seed imbibition and germination rate would have a positive impact in terms of seedling growth and biomass accumulation. To validate the above assumption, the phenotypes of pea seedlings, including length, diameter, biomass, and moisture level, were measured after harvest and summarized in Fig. 4 . In general, the results demonstrated that CDs have a dose-dependent effect on seedling growth and all the groups treated with CDs (0.25–2 mg/mL) had better performance when compared with the control group. As shown in Fig. 4a, the shoot and root lengths of seedlings displayed an increasing trend until the CD concentration for seed priming reached 0.75 mg/mL, then slightly dropped when the concentrations increased to 2 mg/mL. Among all the groups, the one treated with CDs at the concentration of 0.75 mg/mL significantly increased both shoot (P < 0.001) and root (P < 0.01) length when compared with the control group. Regarding the shoot diameter, the group applied with CDs at 0.75 and 1 mg/mL increased by 5.1% and 8.2% than the control group, respectively (Fig. 4b). The root diameters of all the CD treated groups were significantly larger than the control group (Fig. 4b), indicating CD priming may induce the development of stronger root system which is essential in promoting seedling growth. Fig. 4c presents the fresh biomass of shoot and root, where the group treated with CDs of 0.75 mg/mL exhibits the most significant biomass accumulation, which is 29.2% and 19.6% higher than the control, respectively. Similarly, the dry biomass of shoot and root developed from the seeds primed with 0.75 mg/mL CD solution increased by 25.4% and 9.4% compared with the control (Fig. 4d). It can be inferred that CD priming can accelerate seedling biomass accumulation, which is a crucial index during plant growth process. Based on the measurements of fresh and dry biomass, the moisture level of shoot and root was obtained as well. As shown in Fig. 4e, the roots from the seeds exposed to CDs of 0.75 and 1 mg/mL possess prominently higher water content when compared with the control group. The shoot moisture level of groups treated with CDs showed indistinctive difference when compared with the control group (Fig. 4f). The photograph of seedling development after different seed priming treatments is shown in Fig. 4g as a reference, where an obvious difference of phenotype can be observed among the seedlings developed from the seeds primed with water and CD solution. It is worth noting that even under exposure to high concentration of CDs (2 mg/mL) during seed priming, the developed seedlings still exhibit similar or even better performance than control group in terms of the abovementioned phenotypes, demonstrating the low phytotoxicity property of the plastic-derived CDs in this study. Nevertheless, similar with other studies (Li et al., 2016a; Wang et al., 2018), high dose of CDs didn't exhibit beneficial effects in significantly promoting plant growth. One possible reason may be attributed to the lower pH value of CDs with increased concentrations, which disturbed the physiological processes of pea seeds, possibly leading to excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and oxidative stress (Yao et al., 2018). Besides, high concentrations of CDs may tend to form aggregates (Liang et al., 2016) on the seed coat and hamper water uptake process, consequently slowing down seed germination and seedling development. On the other side, when the CD dosage was increased from 0.25 mg/mL to 0.75 mg/mL, the growth indices of pea seedlings increased as well. It is believed that at the low concentration range, with the increase of CD concentrations, more CDs would interact with pea seeds and endow them with accumulative positive influence. According to the results obtained so far, the optimal concentration of CDs for promoting seed germination and seedling growth is considered to be 0.75 mg/mL. To further reveal the impact of CD priming, the subsequent investigations of CD distribution and biochemical indices of pea seeds and seedlings will mainly focus on the group treated with the ideal CD concentration as well as the control group.

It has been verified previously that CDs can penetrate through plant cells and translocate to other plant organelles of mung beans during seed germination (Li et al., 2016a; Wang et al., 2018). However, the uptake and transport of CDs after seed priming is still unknown. To monitor their distribution within pea seeds and seedlings, ultrathin sections of seed coats, embryos, roots, shoots, and leaves were obtained and investigated through commonly adopted TEM technique (Li et al., 2016b; Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b). In the present study, obvious nanoclusters were found in all the abovementioned plant tissues after CD treatment (Fig. 5 ). In addition, pea seeds primed with CD solution possess visible brown color surrounding the hilum area of seed coat (Fig. S2), which is the main entrance of water uptake before seed germination. This finding initially proved the internalization and accumulation of CDs in the pea seed coat and partially supported the corresponding TEM result. Considering the moderately damaged seed coat surface and the hydrophilic property of CDs, it is deduced that CDs may penetrate through the seed coat during seed priming and part of them subsequently enter seed embryo via water imbibition. A portion of the CDs was then transported to roots, shoots, and leaves through the vascular system during water uptake process (Zhang et al., 2018a). Internalized nanoparticles, such as carbon nanotubes, were reported to be responsible for the stimulation of gene expression associated with cell growth and water transport (Khodakovskaya et al., 2009, 2012). According to germination and growth-related traits observed from CD treated groups, it is deemed that CDs in this study may also possess similar capabilities in regulating biochemical parameters and the ultimate quality of mature plants after tissue penetration and translocation. Nevertheless, the translocation of CDs inside plant system may not be the most crucial mechanism that explains the positive priming effects, in view of the limited CD internalization within short priming time. Admittedly, the current study is still insufficient to characterize the accurate translocation of CDs in pea plants. Different from metal-containing NMs, many carbon-based NMs, especially CDs with ultrasmall size, are more difficult to be detected and distinguished among complex environmental matrices (Johnston et al., 2020). The existence of overwhelming natural particles in the plant system that possess similar elemental compositions severely hinders the identification of carbon-based NMs at low concentrations using current analytical methods (Mitrano et al., 2021). Therefore, proper analytical tools are required to be developed for reliable investigation of CDs and continuous endeavors regarding this study will be carried out in the future.

The delicate balance between ROS and antioxidant enzyme system plays a crucial role during seed imbibition and germination (Das and Roychoudhury, 2014). As a normal metabolic product generated within plant system, accumulation of excess ROS would cause undesired oxidative stress that leads to cellular damage or even cellular death when exposed to abiotic and biotic stress (Demidchik, 2015). To ensure the sustainable development of plants, antioxidant enzyme system is evolved to alleviate oxidative stress via ROS scavenging capability. The SOD is a specific enzyme that assists in converting superoxide radical (O2•‾) into H2O2 and molecular oxygen (O2), whereas the enzymes such as CAT, POD, and APX participate actively in the decomposition of H2O2 (Das and Roychoudhury, 2014). In this study, the SOD activity in CD (0.75 mg/mL) primed seeds markedly increased by 63.5% when compared to the control group, implying a high level of O2•‾ in the seed embryo was converted into H2O2 through dismutation (Fig. 6 a). In the meanwhile, the activity of other critical antioxidant enzymes, including POD, APX, and CAT, within CD treated seeds was drastically improved by 80.0%, 46.4%, and 166.7% as compared with those in the water primed seeds, respectively (Fig. 6b–d). The upregulation of the above three enzymes was responsible for the detoxification of redundant H2O2 existed in the seed embryo, leading to the formation of harmless product water (Weydert and Cullen, 2010). According to the above results, it is found that seed priming with CDs can considerably increase the antioxidant enzyme activity. This result also implies that CD priming may induce ROS production within seed embryo, as a result of their interaction with cellular organelles after penetration and internalization (López-Vargas et al., 2020). Although overproduction of ROS molecules may result in oxidative damage, an optimal level of ROS existence, contrariwise, is favorable for plant growth by stimulating various downstream signaling cascades (Nile et al., 2022). It has been reported that the enhancement of antioxidant enzyme activity may effectively increase seed water uptake, facilitate germination rate, and consecutively promote seedling development (Xiao et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2016), which matches well with the phenomenon discovered in the current study. Moreover, elevated activities in SOD, CAT, and APX were found to enhance salt stress resistance of Lathyrus odoratus after halo-priming with silicon (El-Serafy et al., 2021). It is believed that CD priming technique may also hold great potential in endowing plant with better performance in biotic and abiotic stress tolerance, resulting in diminished usage of pesticides and fertilizers and finally contributing to the goal of sustainable agriculture (do Espirito Santo Pereira et al., 2021). Naturally, more research is yet required to unveil the underlying mechanism of CD priming, including the regulation of ROS level inside plant tissues and other changes occur regarding the primary and secondary metabolism of plants.

To examine the effects of CD priming on biochemical indices of the plants, root vigor, carbohydrate content, and chlorophyll content of pea seedlings were assessed. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the root vigor of CD treated groups considerably increases by 52.0% than the control. As a crucial trait for plant growth, root vigor with higher value implies enhanced water absorption capability of the root system, ultimately leading to faster seedling development (Wang et al., 2018). The beneficial effects of CDs on plant phenotypes, including shoot and root length and diameter, biomass accumulation, and root moisture level, are all closely correlated with the increase in root vigor. Fig. 7b demonstrates the comparison of carbohydrate content between the control group and the CD treated group, where the latter is 20% higher than the former. Carbohydrates are the foremost direct organic products of photosynthesis in green plants, serving as essential and primary sources of energy and carbon skeletons for plant growth (Trouvelot et al., 2014). The higher carbohydrate content observed in pea seedlings after CD priming suggested that CDs can potentially elevate photosynthetic efficiency, subsequently promoting plant development. Besides, another photosynthetic index was also evaluated to verify this concept. As shown in Fig. 7c, the chlorophyll contents of all the CD treated groups are significantly higher than that of the control, and reach the maximum value at the optimal CD concentration (0.75 mg/mL) which increases by 18.8%. As an indispensable pigment during photosynthesis process, chlorophyll is responsible for the conversion of light energy into chemical energy fueling for plant growth (Baker, 2008). It can be inferred that CD priming can facilitate the formation of chlorophyll, enhance photosynthetic activity, and increase carbohydrate production, eventually contributing to improved plant growth.

The present study adopted a low-cost, eco-friendly, and facile approach to synthesize CDs using PET bottles through thermochemical conversion followed with H2O2-assisted hydrothermal method. For the first time, plastic-derived CDs were employed as a seed nano-priming agent for the cultivation of green pea, showing beneficial effects on promoting seed germination and seedling development. The germination rates of pea seeds primed with CD solution of all prepared concentrations were noticeably improved compared with the control group. Subsequently, CD treated groups also exerted eminent performance in terms of seedling growth parameters, including shoot and root elongation, biomass accumulation, and root moisture level. It was discovered that the acidic environment created by CD solution was responsible for the surface erosion of pea seed coat, which significantly facilitated water absorption, CD penetration, internalization, and translocation. Moreover, the CD-plant interaction may stimulate multiple metabolic pathways, resulting in enhanced antioxidant enzyme system, increased root vigor, chlorophyll content, and carbohydrate content. Seed nano-priming with CDs could effectively avoid excess NMs exposure in the environment. The synthesis of plastic-derived CDs is another sustainable strategy to alleviate environmental pollution caused by non-biodegradable waste. This study provides new insights into the construction of sustainable agriculture and circular economy via nanotechnology.

Lili Liang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Siew Cheong Wong: Data curation, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Grzegorz Lisak: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
